attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching.
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks to use the Claude API, Anthropic SDKs, or Managed Agents (`/v1/agents`, `/v1/sessions`); user asks to add, modify, debug, optimize, or improve a Claude feature (prompt caching, cache hit rate, adaptive thinking, compaction, code_execution, batch, files API, citations, memory tool) or a Claude model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; or user asks about prompt caching / cache hit rate / cache reads / cache creation in any project that uses the Anthropic SDK (even without mentioning Claude by name).
DO NOT TRIGGER when: file imports `openai`/non-Anthropic SDK, filename signals another provider (`agent-openai.py`, `*-generic.py`), code is provider-neutral, or task is general programming/ML.

content.content:
1	# MHPCO Claim Office Kata
2	
3	## Setting
4	
5	You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
6	Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
7	an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
8	swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
9	moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
10	its venerable traditions.
11	
12	## Feature
13	
14	The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
15	a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
16	damage report against an existing policy.
17	
18	## Rules
19	
20	### Item values and base premiums
21	
22	Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
23	the MHPCO price list:
24	
25	- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
26	- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
27	- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
28	- Potion: 400 G / 40 G
29	
30	Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
31	each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
32	3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.
33	
34	### Premium modifiers
35	
36	- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
37	- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
38	  surcharge.
39	- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
40	  20 % loyalty discount.
41	- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
42	- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
43	- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.
44	
45	All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.
46	
47	### Claim processing
48	
49	A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
50	policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.
51	
52	- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
53	  the damage amount.
54	- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.
55	
56	## Examples
57	
58	The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
59	are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
60	modifiers, unless stated otherwise.
61	
62	### Building block of 3 alike components
63	
64	- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
65	- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
66	- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
67	- 7 runes → 175 G base premium
68	
69	### "Alike" components
70	
71	> ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
72	> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?
73	
74	- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
75	- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)
76	
77	### Modifier scope on multi-item policies
78	
79	> ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
80	> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?
81	
82	- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
83	  amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
84	  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
85	  of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
86	- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
87	  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
88	  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
89	  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
90	  added at the very end
91	
92	### Modifier thresholds
93	
94	- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
95	- sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
96	  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
97	- sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
98	  surcharge applies only if cursed
99	- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
100	  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)
101	
102	### Deductible per damage event
103	
104	- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
105	  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
106	  per damaged item)
107	
108	### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)
109	
110	- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
111	  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
112	- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
113	  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
114	  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)
115	
116	### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material
117	
118	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
119	  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
120	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
121	  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
122	  then deductible: 800 − 100)
123	- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
124	  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
125	  deductible: 500 − 100)
126	
127	### Multiple items of the same type
128	
129	> ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
130	> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
131	> two?
132	
133	- a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000),
134	  cap 4000 G
135	- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
136	  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
137	  separate damage with its own deductible
138	- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
139	  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
140	  sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
141	  whole claim is rejected
142	
143	### Cap exhaustion
144	
145	- a policy covers a sword and an amulet → insurance sum 1600 G
146	  (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
147	- a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
148	  165 G) → cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
149	  premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
150	- a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) → insurance sum
151	  1750 G (= 1000 + 3×250); the block discount affects the premium
152	  only, not the insurance sum
153	- a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
154	  claims of 1500 G each
155	- first claim → payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
156	- second claim → payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
157	  is reduced to the remaining cap)
158	
159	### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor
160	
161	- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
162	  (rounded up)
163	- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
164	  (rounded down)
165	- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
166	  only the final premium or payout is rounded
167	
168	### Edge cases
169	
170	- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
171	- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
172	  `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
173	  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
174	  to stdout
175	- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
176	  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
177	  with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
178	  and writes an error description to stderr
179	- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
180	  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
181	  stderr
182	
183	## Integration examples
184	
185	The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
186	modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.
187	
188	### Newcomer with a cursed sword
189	
190	- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
191	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
192	- premium: 165 G
193	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)
194	
195	### Long-standing customer's second contract
196	
197	> ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
198	> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
199	> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
200	> insurance surcharge still apply?
201	
202	- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
203	  in the scenario
204	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
205	- premium: 160 G
206	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
207	  + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
208	  = 160 G)
209	- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
210	  though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
211	  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
212	  history.
213	
214	## CLI input/output format
215	
216	The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
217	`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
218	scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
219	stdout.
220	
221	A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
222	array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
223	scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
224	operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
225	later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
226	step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.
227	
228	Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
229	and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:
230	
231	- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
232	- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
233	  `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
234	  claim).
235	
236	### Schema example
237	
238	Stdin:
239	
240	```json
241	{
242	  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
243	  "steps": [
244	    {
245	      "op": "quote",
246	      "items": [
247	        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
248	      ]
249	    },
250	    {
251	      "op": "claim",
252	      "policy": 0,
253	      "incident": {
254	        "cause": "fire",
255	        "damages": [
256	          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
257	        ]
258	      }
259	    }
260	  ]
261	}
262	```
263	
264	Stdout shape:
265	
266	```json
267	{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
268	```
269	
270	## Expected Output Files
271	
272	- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
273	- Test files
274	- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)
275	
276	## Constraints
277	
278	- Use TypeScript for the implementation
279	- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
280	- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
281	- Internal data structures and module organization are free
282

file.content:
# MHPCO Claim Office Kata

## Setting

You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
its venerable traditions.

## Feature

The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
damage report against an existing policy.

## Rules

### Item values and base premiums

Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
the MHPCO price list:

- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
- Potion: 400 G / 40 G

Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.

### Premium modifiers

- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
  surcharge.
- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
  20 % loyalty discount.
- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.

All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.

### Claim processing

A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.

- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
  the damage amount.
- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.

## Examples

The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
modifiers, unless stated otherwise.

### Building block of 3 alike components

- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
- 7 runes → 175 G base premium

### "Alike" components

> ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?

- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)

### Modifier scope on multi-item policies

> ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?

- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
  amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
  of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
  added at the very end

### Modifier thresholds

- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
- sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
- sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
  surcharge applies only if cursed
- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)

### Deductible per damage event

- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
  per damaged item)

### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)

- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)

### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material

- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
  then deductible: 800 − 100)
- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
  deductible: 500 − 100)

### Multiple items of the same type

> ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
> two?

- a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000),
  cap 4000 G
- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
  separate damage with its own deductible
- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
  sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
  whole claim is rejected

### Cap exhaustion

- a policy covers a sword and an amulet → insurance sum 1600 G
  (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
- a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
  165 G) → cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
  premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
- a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) → insurance sum
  1750 G (= 1000 + 3×250); the block discount affects the premium
  only, not the insurance sum
- a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
  claims of 1500 G each
- first claim → payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
- second claim → payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
  is reduced to the remaining cap)

### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor

- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
  (rounded up)
- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
  (rounded down)
- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
  only the final premium or payout is rounded

### Edge cases

- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
  `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
  to stdout
- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
  with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
  and writes an error description to stderr
- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
  stderr

## Integration examples

The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.

### Newcomer with a cursed sword

- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
- premium: 165 G
- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)

### Long-standing customer's second contract

> ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
> insurance surcharge still apply?

- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
  in the scenario
- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
- premium: 160 G
- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
  + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
  = 160 G)
- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
  though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
  history.

## CLI input/output format

The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
stdout.

A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.

Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:

- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
  `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
  claim).

### Schema example

Stdin:

```json
{
  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
  "steps": [
    {
      "op": "quote",
      "items": [
        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
      ]
    },
    {
      "op": "claim",
      "policy": 0,
      "incident": {
        "cause": "fire",
        "damages": [
          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
        ]
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

Stdout shape:

```json
{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
```

## Expected Output Files

- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
- Test files
- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)

## Constraints

- Use TypeScript for the implementation
- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
- Internal data structures and module organization are free

input.prompt:
Feature: MHPCO Claim Office - Policy management system with quote and claim operations
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
CLI entry point: src/cli.ts

Requirements (from Example Mapping in prompt.md):

The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for a list of items, and **claim** processes a damage report against an existing policy.

### Item values and base premiums
- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
- Potion: 400 G / 40 G
- Components (runes, moonstones): 250 G each, 25 G base premium per component
- Building block of 3 alike components: special base premium of 60 G

### Premium modifiers
- Cursed items: +50% risk surcharge (item-specific)
- Highly enchanted (enchantment ≥ 5): +30% risk surcharge (item-specific)
- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years): -20% loyalty discount (policy-wide)
- First insurance: +10% initial assessment surcharge (policy-wide, always applies since each quote is treated as first insurance)
- Follow-up contract (not customer's first quote): -15% discount (policy-wide)
- 5 G processing fee added at the very end
- Rounding in MHPCO's favor: premiums round up, payouts round down

### Claim processing
- 100 G deductible per damaged item
- Total payout capped at 2× insurance sum
- Enchantment ≥ 8: reimbursed at 50% of damage
- Dragon material: fully reimbursed
- Both clauses apply: 50% wins, then deductible

### Edge cases
- Empty item list → premium 5 G (processing fee only)
- Unknown item type → non-zero exit, error to stderr
- Claim referencing item not in policy → non-zero exit, error to stderr
- More damage entries than insured items → non-zero exit, error to stderr
- Negative damage amount → non-zero exit, error to stderr

### Examples from the spec:

**Component blocks:**
- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
- 7 runes → 175 G base premium
- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G (no block: different types)
- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G (two separate blocks)

**Modifier scope:**
- cursed sword (100 G) + plain amulet (60 G) → 210 G before fee (curse only on sword)

**Claim examples:**
- regular sword, damage 500 → payout 400 (full minus deductible)
- rune, damage 200 → payout 100
- dragon sword enchantment 9, damage 1000 → payout 400 (50%, then deductible)
- dragon sword enchantment 5, damage 800 → payout 700 (dragon full, then deductible)
- steel sword enchantment 9, damage 1000 → payout 400

**Integration examples:**
- Newcomer, cursed sword: 100 + 50 curse + 10 first = 160 + 5 fee = 165 G
- Long-standing (3yr), second contract, cursed sword ench 7: 100 + 50 + 30 - 20 + 10 - 15 = 155 + 5 = 160 G

**Cap exhaustion:**
- sword insured (cap 2000), two claims of 1500 each: first→1400, second→600

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, no HITL checkpoints.

content.text:
## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

**Refactored Code Mass:**

`calculatePremium` function:
- Bindings: `items` = 1
- Invocations: `items.length` (property access), `===`, `BASE_PREMIUMS[items[0].type]` (bracket lookup), `items[0].type` (property), `basePremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE`, `basePremium + firstInsuranceSurcharge`, `+ PROCESSING_FEE` = 7 x 2 = 14
- Constants: `0` (items.length comparison), `0` (items[0] index) = 2 x 1 = 2
- Conditionals: `if (items.length === 0)` = 1 x 4 = 4
- Bindings: `basePremium`, `firstInsuranceSurcharge` = 2 x 1 = 2
- Assignments: `basePremium = ...`, `firstInsuranceSurcharge = ...` = 2 x 6 = 12

`processScenario` function:
- Bindings: `scenario`, `items`, `premium` = 3 x 1 = 3
- Invocations: `scenario.steps[0]` (property), `calculatePremium(items)` = 2 x 2 = 4
- Constants: `0` (steps[0] index) = 1 x 1 = 1
- Assignments: destructure `items`, `premium = ...` = 2 x 6 = 12

Module-level constants (same as before):
- Constants: `5`, `0.1`, `"sword"`, `100`, `"amulet"`, `60`, `"staff"`, `80` = 8 x 1 = 8
- Bindings: `BASE_PREMIUMS` = 1 x 1 = 1
- Assignments: `BASE_PREMIUMS = {...}` = 1 x 6 = 6

Total Mass: (8 + 2 + 1) + (1 + 2 + 3 + 1) + (14 + 4) + (4) + (0) + (12 + 12 + 6) = 11 + 7 + 18 + 4 + 0 + 30 = **70**

Previous Mass: **67**
Mass Change: 67 -> 70 (delta +3)

The mass increased slightly because we added one new binding (`items` parameter), one new assignment (`premium = calculatePremium(items)`), and one new invocation (`calculatePremium(items)`). This is expected when extracting a function -- it adds structural overhead.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Keep naming: `processScenario` appropriately describes the function's role as a scenario processor.
- Applied: Extracted `calculatePremium` helper function (Rule 2 - Reveals Intent).
- Attempted and reverted: Inline `firstInsuranceSurcharge` -- caused floating-point precision error (`100 * 1.1 = 110.00000000000001`), violating Rule 1.

**Benefits of the extraction**:
- `processScenario` now clearly shows its two responsibilities: (1) extract data from scenario structure, (2) calculate premium. Each is now in a separate function.
- `calculatePremium` is a pure function that takes items and returns a number, making its contract explicit.
- The separation will make future changes cleaner -- adding new step types (e.g., "claim") to `processScenario` won't clutter the premium calculation logic.
- Rule 2 improvement: The code better reveals intent through separation of concerns.

**Trade-off acknowledged**: Mass increased by 3 (67 -> 70) due to function extraction overhead, but Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) trumps APP mass. The improved clarity justifies the small mass increase.

## Step 7: Report Completion

Refactor Phase Complete:
**Refactoring**: Extracted `calculatePremium` helper function to separate premium calculation from scenario parsing. Also attempted inlining `firstInsuranceSurcharge` but reverted due to floating-point precision breakage.
**Mass Change**: 67 -> 70 (delta +3, acceptable for improved clarity)
**Tests**: All 4 passing.

Proceeding to the next test.

Total bindings: 13 x 1 = **13** (removed `items` ref in if-check, `PROCESSING_FEE` ref in early return)

The mass increased slightly, which is expected when extracting concepts into named functions (more bindings and invocations). However, this is a case where **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) trumps APP mass**. The key improvements are:

1. **`isComponentBlock`** -- names the domain concept explicitly. The upcoming tests for 4 runes, 7 runes, and mixed component types will all interact with this concept. The name makes it immediately clear what the condition checks.

2. **`COMPONENT_BLOCK_PREMIUM`** -- separates the block discount premiums into a dedicated lookup, making it extensible (upcoming test for "3 runes + 3 moonstones" with two separate blocks).

3. **`calculateBasePremium`** -- separates the concern of computing the base premium from applying surcharges and fees, making `calculatePremium` cleaner and easier to read.

4. **`basePremium` is now `const`** -- eliminated mutable state. The previous version had `let basePremium` with three possible assignment paths. Now it's a single `const` binding.

5. **Generalized the block check** -- `isComponentBlock` checks `items[0].type` instead of hardcoding `"rune"`, which correctly prepares for blocks of other component types without adding unnecessary complexity.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Naming: Extracted `isComponentBlock` to name the domain concept "building block of 3 alike components" (Rule 2)
- Naming: Extracted `calculateBasePremium` to separate base premium logic from surcharge/fee application (Rule 2)
- Structure: Created `COMPONENT_BLOCK_PREMIUM` lookup to separate block pricing from per-item pricing (Rule 2)
- Simplification: Changed `basePremium` from mutable `let` with 3 assignment paths to immutable `const` (Rule 2 + Rule 4)
- Mass: 98 --> 104 (+6), acceptable increase since Rule 2 (clarity) takes priority over lower mass

Benefits:
- Domain concept "component block" is now explicitly named and testable
- Base premium calculation is separated from surcharge/fee logic
- Mutable state eliminated in `calculatePremium`
- Code is prepared for upcoming block-related tests without over-engineering

- **Refactoring**: Extracted `isComponentBlock` helper, `calculateBasePremium` function, and `COMPONENT_BLOCK_PREMIUM` lookup. Eliminated mutable `let basePremium` in favor of `const`. Generalized block detection to any component type.
- **Mass Change**: 98 --> 104 (+6, justified by Rule 2 taking priority over APP)
- **Tests**: All 7 passing

Wait, re-reading the spec: "item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty, first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base premium (the sum of all item base premiums)."

So the flow is:
1. Calculate each item's base premium
2. Apply item-specific surcharges (cursed +50%, enchantment +30%) to each item's base
3. Sum all item premiums → this is the "policy base premium" that includes item-specific surcharges
4. Apply policy-wide modifiers to the total

content.text:
### Step 5: APP Mass Comparison

**Before Refactoring** (`calculatePremium` function):
```
function calculatePremium(items: any[]): number {
  const basePremium = calculateBasePremium(items);
  const itemSurcharges = calculateItemSurcharges(items);
  return Math.ceil(basePremium + itemSurcharges + basePremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE + PROCESSING_FEE);
}
```
- Bindings: items, basePremium, itemSurcharges = 3
- Invocations: calculateBasePremium(items), calculateItemSurcharges(items), Math.ceil(...) = 3 x 2 = 6
- Assignments (const =): basePremium, itemSurcharges = 2 x 6 = 12
- Constants: none additional (FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE, PROCESSING_FEE are module-level bindings)

Function mass: 3 + 6 + 12 = **21**

**After Refactoring** (`calculatePremium` function):
```
function calculatePremium(items: any[]): number {
  const basePremium = calculateBasePremium(items);
  const itemSurcharges = calculateItemSurcharges(items);
  const firstInsuranceSurcharge = basePremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE;
  return Math.ceil(basePremium + itemSurcharges + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE);
}
```
- Bindings: items, basePremium, itemSurcharges, firstInsuranceSurcharge = 4
- Invocations: calculateBasePremium(items), calculateItemSurcharges(items), Math.ceil(...) = 3 x 2 = 6
- Assignments (const =): basePremium, itemSurcharges, firstInsuranceSurcharge = 3 x 6 = 18

Function mass: 4 + 6 + 18 = **28**

Mass Change: 21 -> 28 (increase of +7)

### Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Naming: All function names already clearly describe their purpose. No renaming needed.
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Extracted `firstInsuranceSurcharge` as an explaining variable in `calculatePremium`. The return statement now reads as a sum of four clearly named components: `basePremium + itemSurcharges + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE`.
- Mass increased by 7 (21 -> 28), but Rule 2 (clarity) trumps APP mass.
- The premium composition is now self-documenting -- each component of the premium formula has a descriptive name, making it immediately clear what contributes to the final premium.

**Trade-off**: The mass increased because we added a binding and an assignment. However, the return statement is now purely a sum of named concepts rather than containing an inline calculation (`basePremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE`). This is a net win for readability, which is the correct trade-off per Simple Design Rules priority order.

### Step 7: Report Completion

**Refactor Phase Complete**:
- **Refactoring**: Extracted `firstInsuranceSurcharge` explaining variable in `calculatePremium` for better intent clarity
- **Mass Change**: 21 -> 28 (acceptable increase; clarity over mass per Rule 2)
- **Tests**: All 12 passing

All 13 tests still passing.

### Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

The change was in `calculateItemSurcharges`:

**Before** (the function only):
- Bindings: `items`, `surcharges`, `item` = 3
- Constants: `0.5`, `5`, `0.3` = 3
- Assignments: `surcharges = 0`, `surcharges += ... * 0.5`, `surcharges += ... * 0.3` = 3
- Conditionals: `item.cursed`, `item.enchantment >= 5` = 2
- Loops: `for item of items` = 1
- Invocations: 0

Function mass before: 3 + 3 + (3 x 6) + (2 x 4) + (1 x 5) + 0 = 3 + 3 + 18 + 8 + 5 = 37

**After** (the function only):
- Bindings: `items`, `surcharges`, `item`, `itemBase` = 4 (+1)
- Constants: `0.5`, `5`, `0.3` = 3
- Assignments: `surcharges = 0`, `itemBase = ...`, `surcharges += itemBase * 0.5`, `surcharges += itemBase * 0.3` = 4 (+1)
- Conditionals: `item.cursed`, `item.enchantment >= 5` = 2
- Loops: `for item of items` = 1
- Invocations: 0

Function mass after: 4 + 3 + (4 x 6) + (2 x 4) + (1 x 5) + 0 = 4 + 3 + 24 + 8 + 5 = 44

Mass Change for function: 37 --> 44 (+7)
Total Mass Change: 191 --> 198 (+7)

The mass increased slightly because we added a binding and an assignment. However, this is a case where **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) trumps APP mass**. The explaining variable `itemBase` makes it clearer that both surcharges are calculated as percentages of the item's base premium, and it eliminates the repeated `BASE_PREMIUMS[item.type]` property lookup, which improves readability.

### Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Naming: All function names already clearly describe their purpose. No changes needed.
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Extracted `itemBase` explaining variable in `calculateItemSurcharges` to clarify that both surcharges (curse and high-enchantment) are percentages of the same item base premium.
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): Removed the duplicate `BASE_PREMIUMS[item.type]` expression (appeared twice in the same loop body).
- Mass: Increased from 191 to 198 (+7) -- mass increase is acceptable because clarity improved (Rule 2 trumps APP).

Benefits:
- Code now clearly shows both surcharges derive from the same base value
- Removing the duplicated property access reduces risk of future bugs (e.g., if someone changes one but not the other)
- More readable within the loop body

### Step 7: Report

Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Extracted `itemBase` explaining variable in `calculateItemSurcharges` to eliminate duplicate `BASE_PREMIUMS[item.type]` and improve clarity
- **Mass Change**: 191 --> 198 (+7, acceptable -- Rule 2 clarity improvement trumps mass)
- **Tests**: All 13 passing

content.text:
## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

The refactoring in `calculateBasePremium` changed:

**Before** (the affected function only):
- `groups: Record<string, any[]> = {}` -- binding (1) + assignment (6) + constant `{}` (1) = 8
- `if (!groups[item.type]) groups[item.type] = []` -- conditional (4) + assignment (6) + constant `[]` (1) = 11
- `groups[item.type].push(item)` -- invocation `push` (2) = 2
- `const group = groups[type]` -- binding (1) + assignment (6) = 7
- `group.length` used twice -- property accesses

**After** (the affected function only):
- `countsByType: Record<string, number> = {}` -- binding (1) + assignment (6) + constant `{}` (1) = 8
- `countsByType[item.type] = (countsByType[item.type] ?? 0) + 1` -- assignment (6) + constant `0` (1) + constant `1` (1) + invocations `??`, `+` (4) = 12
- `const count = countsByType[type]` -- binding (1) + assignment (6) = 7
- `count` used twice -- just binding references

Net change analysis:
- **Removed**: conditional for `if (!groups[item.type])` (-4 mass), assignment `groups[item.type] = []` (-6), constant `[]` (-1), invocation `push` (-2), binding `group` is replaced by `count` (net 0)
- **Added**: `?? 0` nullish coalescing invocation (+2), constant `0` (+1), constant `1` (+1), `+ 1` invocation (+2)
- **Net**: Removed 13, Added 6. **Net reduction: -7 mass**

The refactoring also improves intent: `countsByType` with type `Record<string, number>` immediately communicates "we're counting items per type," whereas `groups` with type `Record<string, any[]>` required reading further to understand that only the array length mattered.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Naming: `groups` renamed to `countsByType` with type changed from `Record<string, any[]>` to `Record<string, number>` (better reveals that only counts matter, not the items themselves)
- `group` renamed to `count` (reveals it's a number, not an array)
- Eliminated the `if (!groups[item.type])` guard and `push` pattern, replaced with simpler increment expression
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): The data structure now directly represents what we care about (counts), rather than storing full items and then only using `.length`
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): Removed the conditional guard for initializing empty arrays and the `push` invocation
- Mass reduction: approximately -7 (removed a conditional, assignment, invocation, and constant; added fewer elements)

## Step 7: Report Completion

Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Simplified `calculateBasePremium` to track counts per type instead of arrays of items, since only the count was ever used. Renamed `groups` to `countsByType` and `group` to `count` for clarity.
- **Mass Change**: ~236 --> ~229 (estimated -7 reduction)
- **Tests**: All 17 passing

Relevant files:
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_15-29-15_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.1-testlist-scope-fix_opus-4-6-portkey-no-thinking-5/src/claim-office.ts`

content.text:
## Step 4 & 5: APP Mass Comparison

**Before refactoring** (magic numbers inline):
- `0.5`, `0.3`, `5`, `0.2`, `2`, `0.15` -- 6 inline constants scattered across function bodies
- These were opaque: a reader had to infer what `0.5` or `>= 5` meant from context

**After refactoring** (named constants):
- `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD`, `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE`, `LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD`, `FOLLOW_UP_DISCOUNT_RATE` -- 6 named constants at module scope
- Same number of elements (6 bindings added, but 6 inline constants replaced by references to those bindings)
- Net mass change is approximately neutral -- 6 new bindings (mass 6) replace 6 constants (mass 6) used in the same positions, but we add 6 new constants for the definitions = +6 mass total

**Mass Change**: Approximately +6 (small increase due to the constant definitions themselves)

However, Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) trumps APP mass. The named constants make the business rules self-documenting.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Naming: Function names reviewed and found adequate -- no changes needed
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Extracted 6 magic numbers into named constants:
  - `0.5` became `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE`
  - `0.3` became `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE`
  - `5` (enchantment threshold) became `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD`
  - `0.2` became `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE`
  - `2` (years threshold) became `LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD`
  - `0.15` became `FOLLOW_UP_DISCOUNT_RATE`
- Mass: Slight increase (~+6), but Rule 2 trumps APP -- clarity wins
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): No duplication found
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): Slight increase in element count justified by intent clarity

**Benefits**:
- Business rules (rates, thresholds) are now documented by their names
- All configurable values are grouped at the top of the file, making them easy to locate and modify
- A reader no longer needs to infer what `0.5` or `>= 5` means from context

## Step 7: Report Completion

**Refactor Phase Complete:**
- **Refactoring**: Extracted 6 magic numbers into named constants for clarity (Rule 2 - Reveals Intent)
- **Mass Change**: ~neutral to slight increase, justified by Rule 2 priority over APP
- **Tests**: All 19 passing

Proceeding to the next test.

**Relevant files:**
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_15-29-15_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.1-testlist-scope-fix_opus-4-6-portkey-no-thinking-5/src/claim-office.ts` (refactored implementation)

content.text:
Let me verify I haven't over-implemented by checking what this test actually needs:

- The test has one quote (sword) and one claim (sword damage 500 G).
- My implementation: stores policy info (items, insuranceSum, remainingCap = 2 * insuranceSum) on quote; on claim, iterates damages, subtracts deductible per damage, updates remaining cap.
- I did include the full `INSURANCE_VALUES` map, which is slightly more than needed for just the sword test, but this follows the same pattern as `BASE_PREMIUMS` which already lists all types. The values are required data, not logic.
- I did NOT implement: enchantment-based reimbursement reduction, dragon material handling, cap clamping, rounding, error handling, or any other future-test features.

This is minimal and appropriate.

---

Green Phase Complete:

**Implementation**: Added `INSURANCE_VALUES` lookup table, `DEDUCTIBLE` constant, and claim processing logic in `processScenario`. When a quote is processed, a policy record is stored with items, insurance sum, and remaining cap (2x insurance sum). When a claim is processed, each damage entry is reimbursed at `damage.amount - DEDUCTIBLE`, the total payout reduces the policy's remaining cap, and the result includes payout and remainingCap.

**Result**: All tests now pass (23 passing, 17 todo)

**Approach**: This is minimal because it only handles the standard deductible case with no special clauses (no enchantment-based reduction, no dragon material handling, no cap clamping, no payout rounding, no error handling). Those features will be driven by future tests.

content.text:
All 23 tests still pass.

## Step 5: APP Mass Comparison (focused on changed section)

**Before -- claim handling inline in processScenario**:
```
const policy = policies[step.policy];    // 1 binding + 1 invocation (property access) = 3
let totalPayout = 0;                     // 1 assignment + 1 constant = 7
for (const damage of step.incident.damages) {  // 1 loop + 1 binding = 6
  const reimbursement = damage.amount - DEDUCTIBLE;  // 1 binding + 1 invocation + 1 binding = 4
  totalPayout += reimbursement;          // 1 assignment = 6
}
policy.remainingCap -= totalPayout;      // 1 assignment = 6
results.push({ payout: totalPayout, remainingCap: policy.remainingCap }); // 1 invocation = 2
```
Inline section mass: ~34

**After -- extracted processClaim function + call site**:
The `processClaim` function has the same internal logic (same mass), but the call site in `processScenario` is now:
```
results.push(processClaim(policies[step.policy], step.incident));  // 2 invocations + property accesses
```
vs the previous 7 lines of inline code.

The total mass is essentially the same (the logic moved, it wasn't eliminated), but the readability improvement is the key benefit here. This is a Rule 2 improvement, not a mass reduction.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Naming: All names already clearly describe their purpose -- no changes needed.
- Structural: Extracted inline claim processing into `processClaim` function (Rule 2 improvement).
- The quote path already delegates to `calculatePremium`; now the claim path consistently delegates to `processClaim`.
- `processScenario` now operates at a uniform level of abstraction: it routes operations to dedicated functions.
- Mass: Approximately unchanged (logic moved, not eliminated). This is a clarity-focused refactoring.

**Benefits**:
- `processScenario` is easier to scan -- each branch is a single function call.
- `processClaim` can be extended independently as more claim tests are activated (enchantment clauses, dragon material, cap logic, etc.).
- Consistent abstraction level in the main orchestration function.

## Step 7: Report Completion

Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Extracted `processClaim` function from inline claim logic in `processScenario` to achieve consistent abstraction levels (Rule 2: Reveals Intent).
- **Mass Change**: Approximately unchanged -- this was a clarity refactoring, not a mass reduction.
- **Tests**: All 23 passing.

Proceeding to the next test.

- Enchantment >= 8: reimburse at 50% → 500

But wait — processClaim doesn't have access to item details (enchantment, material). It only knows the damage amount. The policy stores items, but processClaim needs to match damage entries to policy items to check their properties.

Current processClaim computes: 1000 - 100 = 900. Test expects 400.

content.text:
**Refactored Code Mass for `reimbursementRateFor` + `processClaim` combined**:

`reimbursementRateFor`:
- Constants: 2 (CLAIM_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD, CLAIM_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE, 1) = 3 x 1 = 3
- Bindings: 1 (item) = 1 x 1 = 1
- Invocations: 2 (&&, >=) = 2 x 2 = 4
- Conditionals: 1 (if with &&) = 1 x 4 = 4
- Loops: 0 = 0
- Assignments: 0 = 0
Subtotal: 3 + 1 + 4 + 4 + 0 + 0 = **12**

`processClaim`:
- Constants: 2 (0, DEDUCTIBLE) = 2 x 1 = 2
- Bindings: 4 (totalPayout, damage, matchedItem, item, reimbursement) = 5 x 1 = 5
- Invocations: 8 (incident.damages, find, ===, .type, .itemType, reimbursementRateFor, *, -) = 8 x 2 = 16
- Conditionals: 0 = 0
- Loops: 1 (for...of) = 1 x 5 = 5
- Assignments: 3 (let totalPayout = 0, totalPayout +=, policy.remainingCap -=) = 3 x 6 = 18
Subtotal: 2 + 5 + 16 + 0 + 5 + 18 = **46**

Combined Total Mass: 12 + 46 = **58**

Mass Change: 60 --> 58 (delta -2)

The mass decreased slightly. More importantly, the refactoring improved clarity significantly:
- `processClaim` no longer has an inline ternary with null-checking logic
- The concept of "what reimbursement rate applies to a given item" is now named and encapsulated
- `reimbursementRateFor` is a natural extension point for the upcoming dragon material and threshold-at-boundary tests

### Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Naming: All existing names were adequate. The new helper `reimbursementRateFor` clearly communicates its purpose.
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Extracted `reimbursementRateFor` helper to separate the rate-determination logic from the claim processing loop. The inline expression `matchedItem && matchedItem.enchantment >= CLAIM_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD ? CLAIM_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE : 1` was replaced with a clear function call `reimbursementRateFor(matchedItem)`.
- Mass: Reduced from 60 to 58 (delta -2). The conditional moved to a simpler `if` statement in its own function, eliminating the `&&` short-circuit trick inside a ternary.
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): No duplication found.
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): Added one function (a new element), but this is justified by the clarity gain (Rule 2 trumps Rule 4).

### Step 7: Report Completion

**Refactor Phase Complete**:
- **Refactoring**: Extracted `reimbursementRateFor` helper function from `processClaim` to encapsulate reimbursement rate determination logic.
- **Mass Change**: 60 --> 58 (delta -2)
- **Tests**: All 25 passing.

content.text:
### Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

**Refactored `calculateDamagePayout`**:
```typescript
function calculateDamagePayout(damage: any, policyItems: any[]): number {
  const matchedItem = policyItems.find((item: any) => item.type === damage.itemType);
  return damage.amount * reimbursementRateFor(matchedItem) - DEDUCTIBLE;
}
```

Component Count:
- Constants: 0 = 0
- Bindings: 4 (damage, policyItems, matchedItem, item) = 4
- Invocations: 2 (.find, reimbursementRateFor) = 4
- Conditionals: 0 = 0
- Loops: 1 (find) = 5
- Assignments: 0 = 0

Mass for `calculateDamagePayout`: 13

**Refactored `processClaim`**:
```typescript
function processClaim(
  policy: { items: any[]; insuranceSum: number; remainingCap: number },
  incident: any,
): { payout: number; remainingCap: number } {
  const totalPayout = Math.floor(
    incident.damages.reduce(
      (sum: number, damage: any) => sum + calculateDamagePayout(damage, policy.items),
      0,
    )
  );
  const clampedPayout = Math.min(totalPayout, policy.remainingCap);
  policy.remainingCap -= clampedPayout;
  return { payout: clampedPayout, remainingCap: policy.remainingCap };
}
```

Component Count:
- Constants: 1 (0) = 1
- Bindings: 5 (policy, incident, totalPayout, sum, damage, clampedPayout) = 6
- Invocations: 4 (Math.floor, .reduce, calculateDamagePayout, Math.min) = 8
- Conditionals: 0 = 0
- Loops: 1 (reduce) = 5
- Assignments: 1 (policy.remainingCap -=) = 6

Mass for `processClaim`: 26

**Combined mass** (processClaim + calculateDamagePayout): 26 + 13 = 39

vs. original `processClaim` mass: 35

Mass Change: 35 --> 39 (increase of 4)

### Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Naming: All existing names are clear and descriptive -- no renaming needed.
- Extraction: Extracted `calculateDamagePayout` from the `processClaim` reduce callback (Rule 2 -- Reveals Intent).
- Mass: Increased from 35 to 39 (net +4) due to the additional function signature and invocation overhead.
- Rule 2 trumps APP: The extraction improves clarity. The per-damage calculation (find matched item, apply reimbursement rate, subtract deductible) is now a named, testable unit. The `processClaim` reduce callback is now a simple summation, making the two-level structure clear: (1) calculate payout per damage entry, (2) sum them, floor, and clamp to cap.

Benefits:
- `processClaim` is now easier to read -- the reduce callback is a one-liner.
- `calculateDamagePayout` isolates the per-damage logic (item matching, reimbursement rate, deductible) into a named function whose purpose is immediately clear.
- When future tests add more per-damage complexity (e.g., multiple items of same type), the isolated helper will be the natural place to extend.

### Step 7: Report Completion

Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Extracted `calculateDamagePayout` helper from `processClaim` to improve readability (Rule 2: Reveals Intent).
- **Mass Change**: 35 --> 39 (+4, accepted because clarity improved -- Rule 2 trumps APP).
- **Tests**: All 34 passing.

- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_15-29-15_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.1-testlist-scope-fix_opus-4-6-portkey-no-thinking-5/src/claim-office.ts` (implementation, refactored)

Good. Now let me complete the documentation.

**Step 4: APP Mass Comparison**

Before (relevant section -- the removed `INSURANCE_VALUES` map):
- 6 constants (sword: 1000, amulet: 600, staff: 800, potion: 400, rune: 250, moonstone: 250) = 6
- 6 string constants (key names) = 6
- Total for the map itself: 12

After (the replacement):
- 1 constant (INSURANCE_VALUE_MULTIPLIER = 10) = 1
- 1 invocation (* in the reduce callback) = 2
- Total: 3

Net change on the map: 12 -> 3 (reduction of 9)

However, the usage site changed from `INSURANCE_VALUES[item.type]` (1 invocation = 2) to `BASE_PREMIUMS[item.type] * INSURANCE_VALUE_MULTIPLIER` (2 invocations = 4). That adds 2.

Net mass change: -9 + 2 = -7 (reduction of 7).

**Step 5: Document Decision**

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Naming: All function names accurately describe their purpose. No changes needed.
- Duplication (Rule 3): Removed `INSURANCE_VALUES` constant map, which was a conceptual duplicate of `BASE_PREMIUMS` with a 10x multiplier. Replaced with a single `INSURANCE_VALUE_MULTIPLIER = 10` constant and derived insurance values from `BASE_PREMIUMS[type] * INSURANCE_VALUE_MULTIPLIER`.
- Mass: Reduced by approximately 7 units (eliminated 12 mass from the map, added ~5 mass in usage).

Benefits:
- Eliminates the risk of `BASE_PREMIUMS` and `INSURANCE_VALUES` drifting out of sync when new item types are added
- Makes the 10x relationship between base premium and insurance value explicit and documented
- Fewer constants to maintain (one table instead of two)
- Code is 7 lines shorter

content.text:
The MHPCO Claim Office kata is complete. Here's a summary:

**40 tests passing** covering:
- Quote: empty items, base premiums for all item types (sword, amulet, staff, potion, rune, moonstone)
- Component blocks: 3-alike discount at 60 G, mixed types, various counts
- Item-specific modifiers: cursed (+50%), high enchantment (+30%), both combined
- Policy-wide modifiers: loyalty discount (-20%), first insurance (+10%), follow-up contract (-15%)
- Integration examples: newcomer cursed sword (165 G), long-standing second contract (160 G)
- Premium rounding: Math.ceil (in MHPCO's favor)
- Claims: standard deductible (100 G per item), enchantment >= 8 (50% reimbursement), dragon material (full), both clauses (50% wins)
- Payout rounding: Math.floor (in MHPCO's favor)
- Cap: 2x insurance sum, cap exhaustion across successive claims
- Multiple items of same type with separate deductibles
- Error handling: unknown item types, items not in policy, excess damages, negative amounts

**Files created:**
- `src/claim-office.ts` - Core logic (142 lines)
- `src/claim-office.spec.ts` - Test suite (40 tests)
- `src/cli.ts` - CLI entry point (reads JSON from stdin, writes to stdout)
- `experiment-done.txt` - Completion marker
